[IMAGE:0]It's just a few minutes to midnight, so we can launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  if you ask a question about some occult topic and I tell you it's not something I can or will help you with, and you keep on asking more and more questions about that same topic, don't be surprised if your questions just get deleted. 'Nuf said.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above on the left was my twenty-fourth published book, and in commercial terms it was a total flop. The 2012 hysteria was in full and florid bloom when I started it, and I figured that somebody needed to write a book pointing out, first, that the entire prediction was based on an absurd misunderstanding of the Mayan calendar as filtered through the hallucinogen-soaked brain of drug guru Terence McKenna, and second, that the pattern of belief I called "the apocalypse meme" was one of the most reliable ways to generate bad predictions ever invented in the lifetime of our species. I was foolish enough to think that somebody or other wanted to know this. No such luck; sales were feeble. Of course I was right -- the 2012 business turned out to be an even bigger flop than my book -- but nobody wanted to hear it. It's still a fun little book.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: The Four Creatures
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

My first question is relatively simple: if high magic has as its goal self-transformation, does practical magic have the goal of change in the physical world?

Also, if I wanted to include the four creatures of Ezekiel and Revelations in the SoP, what directions would traditionally be used for them?

And do the four creatures traditionally correspond to the four elements?

Sorry if this seems a little weighty…

Thank you for your help!

replies.title: Re: The Four Creatures
replies.content:
1) Not usually. If you want to change the physical world, it's usually best to use physical methods. Dion Fortune defined magic as the art and science of causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will; since most of the things that make life difficult for people have much more to do with consciousness than with matter, practical magic can fix a great deal, but it does that from the side of consciousness, not from the side of matter.

2 & 3) Those are astrological symbols. The bull is Taurus/earth/north, the lion Leo/fire/south, the eagle Scorpio/water/west, and the man Aquarius/air/east. (Scorpio, alone of the signs, has three animal emblems: the scorpion, the serpent, and the eagle.)

replies.title: Re: The Four Creatures
replies.content:
I apologize,

But would I be right that practical magic deals with the practical side of life? For instance finding a lost object, or healing a skin rash?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: The Four Creatures
replies.content: Yes, and both of those can be dealt with by changes in consciousness: finding the object by recovering the memory of where you left it, healing the skin rash by activating the immune system -- there's an entire science with the jawbreaking name "psychoneuroimmunology" that deals with the interfaces that connect the mind, the nervous system, and the immune system.

replies.title: Re: The Four Creatures
replies.content: This suggests exactly why the Dark Mages used their knowledge of psychoneuroimmunology to lock everyone in their cells, keep them out of sunlight and fresh air, and told them repeatedly that their immune systems would be utterly  impotent in the face of assault the Terrible Terrible Thing....

replies.title: Re: The Four Creatures
replies.content: That possibility has occurred to me rather more than once.

replies.title: Re: The Four Creatures
replies.content: Do you think that the whole thing might have been a working aimed at a mass die off scenario? If so, I'm really, really glad it looks like it didn't work...

replies.title: Re: The Four Creatures
replies.content: I don't know. It may simply have been an attempt to panic as many people as possible by making an ordinary respiratory virus look worse than it was.

replies.title: Re: The Four Creatures
replies.content: I think that part of the whole mess was that The Powers That Be, your "Dark Mages" if you will, became very badly panicked themselves, and so they overreacted hugely.  The initial news from northern Italy reported that every part of the local government had become totally overwhelmed by the massive number of deaths in such a short initial period.  Neither physicians nor morticians could even come close to coping with the initial die-off; the whole system was too inflexible.  And, like every panicked ruler, they desparately imposed stupid draconian rules upon the ruled, lest things escape their control.

comments.title: "On Artificial Gods" Podcast Reccomendation and Scrying in the Long-Term
comments.content:
Good Evening,

A happy early Belteinne/May Day to those of you who mark it this week! For everyone else, may your week bring early flowerings and vitality wherever they're most helpful.

To Share: "John Carter" of Postcards from Barsoom recently appeared on a podcast hosted by a new-to-me blogger named Michael Lindsey, who posts at the substack Thoughtfox. Their discussion was wide-ranging, but its jumping off point was the proposal by a couple of techno-optimist natalists named Malcolm and Simone Collins to craft a new religion that keeps whatever it is about Abrahamic faiths that encourages certain subsets of them to make lots of babies (Amish, Hasidim, etc), while filing away the parts they find icky and/or incompatible with our "destiny in the stars." Well, Carter and Lindsey both had a lot (negative) to say about this project, and ended up getting into some pretty interesting stuff about religion. Enough so that I'm looking forward to diving into Lindsey's archives as soon as I get the chance. Anyhow, if that sounds interesting, check out the discussion here: https://michaellindsey.substack.com/p/on-artificial-gods-with-john-carter

To Ask: I had a question on scrying/pathworking in the long term. This is mostly out of curiosity, as I have plenty of it left to do as part of structured courses of study, but I was wondering: should a mage continue to have some form of scrying as part of his regular practice pretty much as long as he continues to do so? Say, a weekly scrying session and its follow-up meditations? Whether the answer is yes, no, or "it depends on the practitioner," I'd be interested in a few thoughts as to why, if you're willing to share, to help understand the practice's place in overall spiritual development.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: "On Artificial Gods" Podcast Reccomendation and Scrying in the Long-Term
replies.content: It really depends on the practitioner, and also on the specific interests they have in occultism. Scrying is a very useful practice for those who need to develop their imagination, which of course is one of the great working tools of the mage, but it can be overdone. Divination and reflection are useful guides here.

replies.title: Re: "On Artificial Gods" Podcast Reccomendation and Scrying in the Long-Term
replies.content: As for the new pseudoreligion, rationalists have been trying that sort of gimmick for a couple of centuries now. It always fails, because the people who try it never actually believe in the religion they're pushing -- they just want other people to believe in it, and that gives it a vibe of hypocrisy that most people can smell at fifty paces. To found a religion you have to believe in it yourself, and do so passionately enough that you're ready to die for your faith. Nothing else is even close to adequate.

replies.title: Re: "On Artificial Gods" Podcast Reccomendation and Scrying in the Long-Term
replies.content:
1) On your answer to the question above, thank you, that makes sense. Mostly I got to it from the very prosaic reflection of projecting how long it will take me to get through various sources of meditation themes if I'm devoting four days a week to a scrying and the minimum three follow-up meditations. Not that I'm in a hurry, just trying trying to get a handle on it.

2) That's pretty close to the heart of the critique both Carter and Lindsey bring to it, but in elaborating on it, they get into some territory I, at least, found interesting, though I of course there were elements I disagreed with in what they got into. Still a fun, listen, though.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: "On Artificial Gods" Podcast Reccomendation and Scrying in the Long-Term
replies.content:
> just want other people to believe in it

I expect that this is a mischaracterization of the Collinses' motives. I think the idea is more like, everyone involved is meant to be fully aware (at least, other than during the states of mind resulting from ritual) that the rationale for having the religion this way is explicitly so that the traits they all mostly value don't get demographically outcompeted.

I missed my chance to do more than skim bits of "The Pragmatist's Guide to Crafting Religion" when a housemate had it out, but I get the impression that one of the things the Collinses want to protect from getting outcompeted is people's tendency to believe true things about straightforwardly factual questions like e.g. the material history of the Earth, when the alternative is religious cosmogonies whose plain (and, thus, inevitably-perennially-reinvented-by-fundamentalists) readings contradict basic facts about the material history of the Earth. That being the case, why would the Collinses ever want to make a religion whose demographic fitness advantage only manifested when the believers had factual beliefs which the Collinses considered to be false? After all, that would be directly shooting themselves in the foot with respect to their actual goal. But then if the Collinses only wanted people (including their children) to believe in a religion whose factual claims were true, then in what sense could it be the case that they "never actually believe[d] in it"?

With respect to religious claims about "non-factual" questions, I guess I was imagining that someone in the Collinses' position would just treat such claims operationally in terms of their implications for ritual orthopraxy and observance of religious precept. Like, this would be the same sort of way that Shinto largely defines itself as being an observance of certain practices rather than as being a system of beliefs about about cosmology. Maybe the Collinses haven't come up with any such claims; as of the last report I read, they just identify as "secular Calvinists" and their current strategy is to "intentionally craft and adopt secular cultural attitudes that provide exogenous [i.e. not from within the parents' own motivations] reasons to reproduce beyond replacement rate".

So, interestingly, I guess what the Collinses consider to be for other people is not some religion they invented, but the parts of their book about "crafting religion" that are actually about religion as such, rather than just about culture in general. The Collinses don't "actually believe" that religion is the best option by their lights for their family. But if someone else wants, perhaps mostly for other reasons, to pass on a religion to their descendants, then the Collinses "want" (those) "other people to believe" true things about what it would take to make that religion demographically successful -- the same things that the Collinses themselves believe about what it would take to make their own "secular cultural attitudes" demographically successful.

As for the podcast, the part I liked most was at the end (01:42:55):

> Carter: I think, essentially, you know -- The tagline to my substack is "Awaiting the New Hierophany". And this is the sort of attitude of, "Gelassenheit", if you like, that I think people should adopt. Which is that we need to stop thinking of things as coming from us; and we need to practice an openness to --
>
> Lindsey: Openness! Yes! Yes.
>
> Carter: -- the new revealing of the world --
>
> Lindsey: Yes. Abso-was just about to say. Yep. Receptivity.
>
> Carter: Yeah. -- and to "let the century come to us", as it were.
>
> Lindsey: Yes, the receptivity. Exactly. Yes. If-te-that's -- i[f] you -- Maintaining a stance of openness, of... you know, of, of... not trying to force it; not trying to build it...
>
> Carter: Mm-hmm.
>
> Lindsey: ...But allowing it to -- present itself to you. -- When it counts.

(Also, the "Abso-was" at 01:43:18 is kind of interesting to transcribe. If you listen to it, partway through enunciating it, Lindsey cut over from trying to say "Absolutely" to trying to say "I was", sort of like what a sound engineer would call a crossfade. But instead of being on the level of sound, the crossfade was on the level of Lindsey's low-level motor planning of his speech articulation. So when Lindsey tried to pronounce the word "was", the part about the "s" tried to flag itself as already having been completed, because Lindsey had already pronounced the "s" in "Absolutely" and there wasn't any other "s" in "I was". But that flagging didn't fully go through, so then there was this resulting only mostly-blank spot at the end of Lindsey's "was". So the blank spot borrowed a little bit of the pronunciation from the next fricative consonant up, which was the "j" at the beginning of "just". The overall result, considered in terms of phonetics, was like "aaibswej". The weird thing is that our brains automatically pick up on the intentions behind the fumble of the "s", and invisibly transpose the "s" two phonemes forward, to the end of "was", without us really noticing unless we go back and think about it. I only noticed because I realized that I had unthinkingly made two different transcriptions, "Absolutely" and "I was", of the same part of Lindsey's dialogue. I guess this is related to those neuroscience results about how there's a lot of automatic cognitive illusory time-shuffling constantly going into the subjective experience of temporal sequencing of sensory experiences.)

replies.title: Re: "On Artificial Gods" Podcast Reccomendation and Scrying in the Long-Term
replies.content: That's even worse. A deliberately chosen ideology is not a religion, and dressing it up in the borrowed duds of faith doesn't make it one. Religions are born from human confrontations with the transcendent, "the uncontrollable mystery on the bestial floor" in Yeats's neat poetic phrase; they are always rooted in the irrational and the incomprehensible. There have been plenty of attempts to do what the Collinses are trying to do, and they always fail miserably, because human beings aren't rational creatures and a rationally selected ideology doesn't have the power over emotion and action that a living encounter with the transcendent brings with it.

replies.title: Re: "On Artificial Gods" Podcast Reccomendation and Scrying in the Long-Term
replies.content:
I looked at one of their essays. I guess a lot of their concern is actually that diversity of culture, both religious and secular, was going to decrease under modern conditions, rather than just that people with traits they liked were going to demographically collapse. The approach I am seeing is significantly less High Modernist and somewhat closer to triumphant-narrative-exhausted post-modernist unsentimental pragmatic searches for lots of tweaks to apply at once to systems, even if those systems are ones where it feels too taxing to even really think about trying to fully understand them. Less like a monoculture farm plot, and more like looking empirically at volunteer plants and nearby wilderness for ideas for experimental redesigns to try with various existing practices mostly of high intensity mixed gardening. Less like a centralized command economy that ignores all the wisdom of the market, and more like using insights from the axiom-based-derivation-guided theory of economic mechanisms to design an auction-house that takes what wisdom of the market is locally available and makes the most one can of it.

We have that Lot and Jacob did both contest with YHWH. It's not a one-sided process. This attitude of contempt that you're advocating towards the Collinses' methods doesn't seem to differentiate between degrees of those methods, or better or worse aspects of those methods. But to me, their methods are just a concentration of a certain form of common-sense restraint and concern for consequence, and not even that much of a concentration. So it's hard for me to extrapolate your implied advocacy and emotional endorsement into a principle other than just rolling over and helplessly letting each religious inspiration, and the various phenomena that happened to ride into existence alongside that religious inspiration for purposes of its material manifestation, do whatever they want. If the only available course of action that avoided a collective obliteration of the memory of our evidence for, say, the broad idea that about four and a half billion years ago the earth condensed out of a dust cloud under the action of gravity and was afterwards shaped by geology, was condemned by someone as "technocratic managerial methods", how much would you want to let that sway you from at least trying to find a way to make it work?

replies.title: Re: "On Artificial Gods" Podcast Reccomendation and Scrying in the Long-Term
replies.content:
A friend recently introduced me to their channel. Odd couple indeed. Their overall goal seems to be to "Make Progress Great Again." It seems they came to the realization that we're not going to make it to the stars if the people who want to make it to the stars are massively outbred by those who couldn't give a hoot about making it to the stars. Thus their odd progressian version of pro-natalism. Simone's Aldous Huxley glasses is the cherry on top in terms of their overall aesthetic. This might play into the idea that the more avant garde among the coastal PMC elite is going to soon adopt a 1930s fashion sense (may the gods help us if that is taken to its logical conclusion).

Of course I agree with the observations above that their approach to religion will fail right out of the gate. They seem like typical atheists to me and their real view on religion seems to be that it's a time-tested form of crowd control technology that should be used again for keeping the rubes in line. And in general, once you have to explain religion in rational terms, it's already failed as a religion; instead, it's mere ideology. Even worse is redefining religion entirely for utilitarian purposes. I notice the latter impulse expressed quite a bit on a few alt-right blogs I lurk, i.e. "Well, Christianity is the best method we have for getting women back into the kitchen and birth-rates back above replacement rate, so let's do that again, and ohh...here's a bunch of cherry-picked passages from the Old Testament that can make that happen." I noticed that a good portion of the non-establishment right is simply a competing sect of progressivism. Their main beef with the left is that it empowers and emboldens (what they see as) the genetically-unfit in adopting them as political clientele; it's a major roadblock preventing us from getting to the stars.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Huge amounts of sleep are incredibly beneficial for any kind of deep healing. When our conscious minds go to sleep, the various parts of our souls are temporarily left unhindered by its far more inept attempts at forcing a rebalancing within us. When we're awake, a huge amount of the soul's innate healing competency gets expended on chasing after the conscious mind to keep its well-intentioned but befuddled antics from causing too much damage to the larger Self. Our eternal soul knows so much more than our finite consciousness could possibly learn in one lifetime, so the vast soul will always be much better suited to healing any incomprehensibly overwhelming injuries.

On the other hand, our conscious mind will be awake and up to mischief for a considerable portion of each day. Whenever sleep is not a viable option, feeding one's soul and keeping one's conscious mind preoccupied with and flooded by immersive emotional, imaginative, or contemplative experiences is a divine way to allow one's soul to continue its deep rebalancing of the larger Self relatively undistracted. Those experiences can also allow a spontaneous means for interested divinities to aid our souls in their healing work. Music and massage are both brilliant immersive non-rational ways to feed our souls' innate gifts. Dance in any form is another, whether as a Surya Namaskar or Tai Chi sequence or the ecstatic trembling of the Shakers.

May all your soul's dreams unfold to the music of the spheres in their eternal dance.

— Christophe

comments.title: Castaneda and Flyers
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A few weeks ago (I think) you mentioned something about Carlos Castaneda's books being fiction, but that he clearly had some background or understanding of sorcery.

I am curious about your views of the cosmic predators that he called "the fliers", Do they exist, and do they "control" humans? I always thought that this was a particularly weird plot twist in his books, but maybe you have different thoughts. Here's an excerpt where (I think) they were first introduced:

“[Long ago, the native sorcerer/shamans of Mexico] discovered that we have a companion for life,” he said, as clearly as he could. “We have a predator that came from the depths of the cosmos, and took over the rule of our lives. Human beings are its prisoners. The predator is our lord and master. It has rendered us docile; helpless. If we want to protest, it suppresses our protest. If we want to act independently, it demands that we don’t do so.[...] They took over because we are food for them, and they squeeze us mercilessly because we are their sustenance. Just as we rear chickens in chicken coops, gallineros, the predators rear us in human coops, humaneros. Therefore, their food is always available to them.

[...]“In order to keep us obedient, meek and weak, the predators engaged themselves in a stupendous maneuver- stupendous, of course, from the point of view of a fighting strategist; a horrendous maneuver from the point of view of those who suffer it. They gave us their mind! Do you hear me? The predators give us their mind which becomes our mind. The predators’ mind is baroque, contradictory, morose, and filled with the fear of being discovered any minute now.

“I know that even though you have never suffered hunger,” he went on, “you have food anxiety which is none other than the anxiety of the predator who fears that any moment now its maneuver is going to be uncovered, and its food is going to be denied. Through the mind, which after all is their mind, the predators inject into the lives of human beings whatever is convenient for them. The predators ensure in this manner a degree of security to act as a buffer against their fear.”

“An incomplete person has a hole in the stomach,” she went on. “A sorcerer can see it as plainly as you can see my head. When the hole is on the left side of one’s stomach, the child who created that hole is of the same sex. If it is on the right side, the child is of the opposite sex. “Can you see that hole in anyone who has had children?”
“Sure. There are two ways of seeing it. A sorcerer may see it in dreaming or by looking directly at a person. A sorcerer who sees has no problems in viewing the luminous being to find out if there is a hole in the luminosity of the body. replies.title: Re: Castaneda and Flyers

The 1970s were the golden age of "faction," fictional narratives presented as fact, and they made fantastic amounts of money. Some of the most famous books of the time -- the multiple-personality classic Sybil, the drug-abuse narrative Go Ask Alice, the ghost thriller The Amityville Horror, and the Castaneda books among them -- belonged to that category, wholly fictional but presented as fact.

As for Castaneda, his works were always a mixed bag. I never read the posthumous ones -- the last one I read was The Second Ring of Power, and it didn't impress me the way the first three did.

Morning John

replies.title: Re: Gender
replies.content:
First of all, you're quite correct -- the individuality is neither male nor female. Being one or the other gender is part of incarnation, imposed on us by the biology of our material bodies as part of the process of gaining experience in matter.

As far as gender dysphoria goes, I think that in many cases it's an effect of spillover of unconscious memories from one life into the next, caused by too short a time out of incarnation. That's why there's so much of it now, since there are so many human bodies to fill and so souls are rushed from one life to the next. They don't have anything like enough time to process the experiences of one life before they land in another, and so their new body feels wrong.

replies.title: Re: Gender
replies.content:
I have a form of (undiagnosed) male to female gender dysphoria and would like to offer that as I have dug down into my own experiences I'm finding a lot of my issues have to do with our culture's clueless mismanagement of astral images in this incarnation rather than bleed over from previous incarnations. Granted, I don't have past life memories, so there could always be more to it I'm missing.

I am a male who grew up at a time when "Girl power" was all the rage. Parents were imagining what an achievement it would be to raise their daughters as the family's first doctor or CEO and therefore all the buzz was about encouraging specifically women to make the most of their lives. Sons succeeding was not notable in the popular imagination - we were commonly assumed to be able to ride the patriarchy to wherever we happened to end up. The media image of "masculinity" was typified in Tim Allen's repulsive performance on the sitcom Home Improvement: man children making monkey noises while being fascinated with power tools and other "powerful" toys which they aren't bright enough to use responsibly and end up making a mess.

My own parents, on the other hand, kept the traditional male pressure and expectations on me to do something with my life while also being very strict and not giving me the emotional security to assert myself. So in one of the supreme ironies of the universe, my treatment as a male by my conservative upbringing coincided more with the cultural zeitgeist of my driven but reserved female peers than it did with my experience of the directionless class clowns that a lot of boys my age became.

As a result, I associate a lot of traditionally masculine traits with women. I associate women with being peers rather than lovers to the extent that when media tries to offer me eye candy, I find myself asking "would I want to be that person?" rather than "would I want to be with that person?" And I've had to spend most of my last decade sorting out the strong female charge that my strict meritocratic upbringing imparted to my astral body - being told to shut up and perform focuses the mind on being desired by another's standards (female astral body), not actively desiring and working toward your own ends (male astral body).

Getting through a lot of this has definitely involved OP's observation that the individuality is beyond gender. Most of the traits I call male or female really belong to the third gender of "well-adjusted adult" and it has been a matter of digging them out from under all the associations and moving them to the right bin so I can recognize them in other well-adjusted adults rather than just women.

replies.title: Re: latent human abilities?
replies.content:
There's enormous confusion these days about what magic can or can't do. In my experience, magic does not violate the laws of physical nature -- I've never seen anybody levitate an object, for example -- and practices such as remote viewing get so many more misses than hits that it's hard to say what's going on.

That doesn't mean that magic is useless -- it means that it's a bad idea to try to use a hammer as though it was a saw! Magic, in Dion Fortune's useful definition, is the art and science of causing change in consciousness in accordance with will. Most human problems are caused by inadequate or unbalanced consciousness. Unless you're locked away in solitary confinement, for example, you have plenty of opportunities to make money and plenty of opportunities to have happy love affairs -- you just don't see those opportunities, or if you see them, your state of consciousness keeps you from making good use of them. Change your consciousness and you change your life.

It's also possible to get a little spookier and develop intuitive abilities that will guide you to what you need and want. You can learn to be at the right place to meet the right people, for example, and avoid the wrong places and the wrong people. You can also know from personal experience that birth isn't a beginning and death isn't an end, and you can open your mind to subtle perceptions that will sometimes let you hear people's thoughts and sense the future before it happens.

All these things are normal for human beings. They're not supernatural or abnormal; we can all learn to do them. The difficulty is that our entire culture is committed to trying to convince us otherwise. At the core of modern industrial culture is the claim that you can never be more than a cog in the corporate machine. That's why people are taught that magic doesn't work -- and it's also why the mass media stuffs their heads full of claims that magic is only for special people, a la the Harry Potter schlockfest, and that it does things that in reality it can't do.

comments.title: Temple technology update
comments.content:
Hi All

I thought it timely to provide an update on my temple technology research. Basically, it's a far more exciting field with depth, flavour, and history to match any other esoteric discipline, with the added difference that research in it is the equivalent of opening an ancient, but relatively intact ruin, but written in another language, and indeed, in another way of thinking that is completely foreign to modern minds. I know there's a few people here interested in it, and I am still writing two papers on it (one on the radiophysics side, and another on the subtle dimensions), but I thought I would update people:

*Any indepth study into the inner dimensions of most science and technical disciplines will arrive at the temple technology, if one has the keys. The keys were largely lost. Architecture, music, radio, and gardening find a ready path to the tech if people are willing. Yes, a low energy link between all those disciplines is a pretty exciting thing for the future we face.

*In the West, French and the Germans (from the 1930s to the 1970s) painstakingly pieced the fundamentals of it back together based on the Chinese and Egyptian traditions (the Egyptians were the most advanced).

*It was nearly lost again as a generation died, but then synchronicity struck, the research library passed into the hands of a young Egyptian architect and polymath, Dr Ibrahim Karim. He, his family, and students, have spent the next 50 years building on where the French got to (and avoiding the dangers), under the discipline of BioGeometry (biology/earth/measuring). Whilst this is only a component of the temple tech, it appears to be the most active public lineage, and is actively taught around the world, including in the US (by Dr Robert Gilbert, of North Carolina). The courses are a bit pricey for my current budget, but Dr Karim has published extensively and so I'm picking up his take on the tradition that way.

*There's a string of other researchers who have worked on the fringes on this stuff, and there's a particularly interesting Russian line, starting with Prof N A Kozyrev,and continuing to this day. The Russians don't seem to have as many hang-ups about overturning assumptions if the data shows itself to be correct and repeatable.

*The temple tech definitely works on the life force, however it manifests itself. Shapes, particularly the platonic shapes, have different qualities and effects on other life forms. Some shapes, namely hemispheres, cones, and pyramids, have very special properties, some of them highly dangerous. This applies to as far as the shape and wavelength of radio waves and wave forms, but the temple tech is not a new form of radiowave (but these can be used in that context).

*Most of the ancient structures that show aspects of the temple tech appear to be well-understood radio devices. So far, I've found aspects of dielectric resonance antennas, low frequency masers, and piezoelectric acoustic couplers in the famous monuments. There will be more. The primary requirement is resonance with something.


*However there's a critical feature, most of the ancient monuments and temples are not made out of conductive material. They are made from insulators - eg limestone. Maxwell's equations won't model these (I was stuck by this for over a year). It's only recently that science has figured out that through symmetry breaking, you do not need conductors to make antenna. Any object, based on its external and internal structure down to the crystal level, is a radio device of some description. The radiesthesists speak of 'the rupture of the forces', and it seems science has caught up:

https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.114.147701
https://spectrum.ieee.org/gigahertz-antenna-on-a-chip

*It's far broader than the diamagnetism/paramagnetism design, although that does amplify effects.

*The temple tech can be understood in current physics, with biophotons that stream from various devices (referred to as emitters) able to be detected by modern equipment (as opposed to special pendulums). This is the road Dr Phil Callahan went down, although he was substantially acquainted and aware of the French and German radiesthesia traditions, in fact, so aware I suspect he was a natural radiesthesist, who could detect stuff without a great deal of equipment (he called it "tuning into nature"). Dr Callahan's interest was in the radiophysics side and building devices that worked without needing the extensive training in radiesthesia.

*Dr Karim and others speak of a 'physics of quality', and trying to align the modern obsession with quantity (almost all modern science is focused on quantitative analysis) with a physics that assess quality. Apparently this what ancient cultures knew instinctively, and we don't.

*Understanding both provides an ability to transmute 'bad' (for humans) into 'good', through the design of structures. This may also extend to 2D drawings (think radionics), sounds, and words, and critically, test for it.

Heady stuff, and I can understand if its a little intense and full on. One of the French radiesthesists did advise against the excitement he called "kaleidoscopy", where you'd be lost in a world of new colours.

But I thought I'd provide it, as that is the result of quite a few years of intense study, and these themes may guide others in their work.

replies.title: Re: Temple technology update
replies.content:
To be clear, Sinha and Amaratunga are using the term "symmetry breaking" incorrectly, and you should be careful to clarify this when reporting on their their results. Normally the term "symmetry breaking" refers to a process where you set up a physical system in a symmetric way, such as placing a ball on the exact top of a perfectly conical mound, and it's the system itself that pushes itself out of that symmetric state to one of multiple possible asymmetric states (chosen symmetrically at random), such as the ball rolling down a random side of the mound. That's why the term uses the verb "breaking" in the present-progressive tense.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symmetry_breaking

The antenna designs they describe have the symmetry "broken" in advance by the designers, and only just in that the designers are using parts that are conventionally used with symmetric connections, but with asymmetric connections instead. They should just have used some variant of the word "asymmetric".

I don't know what happened during the composition or editorial review of the IEEE Spectrum article, but I think the claim about Maxwell's equations is incorrect or very misleadingly stated. Dielectric substances do have significant net motions of electrons, meaning that there is no mystery as far as Maxwell's equations are concerned. It's just that the electrons are moving from one side of their respective atoms or small molecules to the other side, rather than passing through a conductive medium in an untethered way. It's still a legitimate current of electrical charges. The problem is just in your assumptions as to whether or not there was anything that would count as an electrical current for purposes of Maxwell's equations. If you missed that point, then you should adjust your confidence levels in your own understanding of the rest of the mundane physics involved accordingly as well.

Also, while I don't have access to Sinha and Amaratunga's paper, and I'm relatively far from being an electrical engineer, I suspect that it's not all that great as a demonstration of the dielectric resonator antenna phenomenon.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dielectric_resonator_antenna

The pairs of interdigitated contacts on the piezoelectric filter in their experiment are conductors, and it looks from the figure as though their cross-sections and projected diameters averaged over directions are comparable to those of the active parts of the piezoelectric element themselves. Maybe you could get a comparable effect just from having some same-shaped conductive patches floating in free space, connecting them with two capacitors in series that are both much smaller than the antenna, and applying a waveform to one of the capacitors, using the two contacts themselves as effectively two ends of an antenna. In that case the capacitors would be serving a function similar to that of the piezoelectric element, and they'd be too small to couple to external fields, meaning that it doesn't seem like the piezoelectric element itself is carrying all that much of the load of electromagnetic coupling to external fields.

Dielectric, diamagnetic, and paramagnetic components are "passive" and linearly responsive to radio energy; they can act as waveguides and resonators, but radio energy isn't something that they can permanently transform to or from other forms (besides transforming it into heat) or even just other frequencies. Nor is radio energy something that they can increase the coherence of, by concentrating it into a smaller étendue (direction*area region of focus) starting from a larger one. If you want to use such passive components to make a space where radio energies are concentrated, you have to couple the components to a source that you know to have a small étendue whose location in direction*area-space will be consistent, or will be well-predicted by mechanical parts to keep the passive components lined up with the source. If you want to transform radio energies to or from other forms of energy at the same frequency, you need other kinds of coupling elements like piezoelectric components (to transduce with mechanical vibrations) or conductors (to transduce with electrical currents). And to change the frequency of radio energy, pump it with non-oscillating forms of energy, or convert it back and forth in such a way as to concentrate it in étendue, you need components with nonlinear responses.

"Eloptic" phenomena sound very much to me like they are actually not radio-frequency electromagnetic phenomena, so it should not be surprising that there would be ways around needing to use electrical conductors to manipulate them, or to do nonlinear things like changing their frequency or concentrating them in étendue. If you missed my earlier amateur speculations about how the description of eloptic or etheric energies might correspond to non-cylindrical Kaluza-Klein excitations or rulial-coupled excitations, you might want to check them out. I don't have the links handy, but I think they were in replies to some of your previous comments.

replies.title: Re: Temple technology update
replies.content:
Thank you for your thoughtful response

I wasn't presenting Sinha and Amaratunga's paper as the answer or a guide on dielectrics or the dielectric resonance antenna. The physical properties of these are well known.

It's explicitly about symmetry breaking and a forgotten feature of Marconi's original antenna (which, incidentally, was an inverted pyramid waveguide). Now it may be that symmetry breaking isn't needed to explain it, that you can explain it with your approach, but if you do that, you are basically accepting that wavelength may not matter in radio broadcast the way we thought it did - that in working at least with the magnetic component of a wave, it isn't as relevant. If wavelength doesn't matter as much, then resonance does.

The trouble I see with the dielectric approach is this - technically everything has a dielectric constant, or permittivity, between 1 (perfect vacuum) and infinity, and if everything is a dielectric of a form, then yes, everything is a radio receiver of a form, and everything may also be a transmitter. That is a fantastic statement, one most people won't accept. It has been disregarded on the basis that small things and small currents have no effect, but if they do (and radiesthesia shows that they do), then how we think about electromagnetic energy changes entirely. We are floating in spectrum, and that can have an effect on life. BioGeometry and the radiesthesia tradition takes that approach.


Whilst I think that is true, we are so used to high energy radio it won't be accepted - we aren't used to dealing with whole spectrum. The usual approach to that everything or nothing issue is to apply a gauge on the equations of various forms - frequency being the most common - as traditionally radio devices are assessed based on their use, which is talking to each other in a wide or narrow waveband, and so the equations are used in this way.

Take a large standing stone, or a temple, or something similar. It is receiving energy across the entire spectrum, irrespective of frequency, and transducing it. The effect of any transmitted energy, usually into the ground, will be a combination of the area of the shape and the properties of the shape. That energy may not be coherent to us, or for our commonly understood principles, but it is still occurring.

Those ancient structures, such as the Holy of Holies in the Temple of Solomon, have resonance chambers which correspond to the fundamental Schumann resonance of 7.8hz. That is an oscillator at the heart of the bigger structure, and as a result, the whole structure is a transceiver. It's also a maser (or the sound analog, a saser) in the sense that stimulated emission within the structure may occur (particularly in response to singing or chanting), with emissions going straight out the eastern door on the mainline of the structure. The similarities between the design of a hydrogen maser and the Temple of Solomon are uncanny.

Also, they aren't passive. Any substance that receives electromagnetic energy will emit photons (or phonons, in the context of any piezoelectric effect) in response to that energy, usually into the ground. That is probably dismissed as heat, but the character of that emission can be measured, and has an effect on life.

And so we wind up back at the old aether issue, if we pull the gauge out of various equations, we get back to the aether.

I will look up the non-cylindrical Kaluza-Klein excitations or rulial-coupled excitations you cite.

replies.title: Re: ET Life etc
replies.content:
I wouldn't be at all surprised if there were countless intelligent species scattered across the universe. That doesn't mean they're on their way to us, since there are good reasons to think that interstellar travel isn't possible. I covered some of those in this post back in my former blog:

https://thearchdruidreport-archive.200605.xyz/2007/09/solving-fermis-paradox.html

Other beings on planets of this solar system, if they exist, are not biological entities or "life forms" -- life, if that word is to be taken in its usual sense, can only exist in a very narrow range of environments, and nowhere else in the solar system outside Earth do those environments exist. (The harshest environments on earth -- the bottom of the Marianas trench, the top of Mount Everest, the central Antarctic plain in winter, the middle of the deadly Takla Makan desert -- are better suited to life than the most mellow environment on any other planet or moon in the solar system.) If you want to talk about intelligences that don't have biological bodies, on the other hand, there's an old and useful term for such beings: "spirits."

Do those exist? Sure, and human beings have been interacting with them since the dawn of our species. Our modern industrial culture is almost uniquely clueless in its relationship with the realm of spirits; most other cultures have established methods for managing that relationship, which are called "religion" and "magic." Does that answer your questions?

replies.title: Re: ET Life etc
replies.content: Yes, that certainly goes a long way in answering my questions. I use the term EBE (extra-biological entities) precisely for the reasons you cite on the limitations of what we call biology -- life-forms inhabiting the densest (physical) plane (which often simultaneously transcend it as well). I agree that our modern industrial society is uniquely clueless about "finer matter" (beyond the "empirically verifiable" physical/sense-perceptible plane) and that many ancient and pre-modern cultures globally indeed took for granted the existence of higher worlds and communication with them. Given those premises, my follow-up inquiry is whether you believe communication with these "trans-physical" beings on other planets/systems takes place nowadays, and specifically what you think is going on with the many self-described "channelers" of such beings and the information/teachings the latter allegedly are imparting. Is any of it authentic, to be taken seriously?

replies.title: Re: ET Life etc
replies.content: Channeling used to be called mediumship, and the spirits who communicate through it will pretend to be whatever's fashionable. In the early days they claimed to be dear Aunt Mildred who died a few years ago; then they started claiming to be Native American chiefs, or Tibetan masters, or what have you; these days a fair number of them claim to be aliens from Zeta Absurdii. It's always the same schtick. They don't know anything the channeler doesn't know -- they've got access to the inside of that person's mind, no question -- but most of them don't have anything to offer other than high-grade handwaving. Such spirits are good at telling people what they want to hear, though, so they always get an audience.

comments.title: The meaning of Ordeal
comments.content:
Greetings, JMG and all!

I'm currently working on a book on the Heathen concept of Orlog, roughly (very roughly) translated as 'fate,' and part of my discussion also focuses on the concept of the 'ordeal,' a word that I find fits well into the domain of Orlog. Orlog is, linguistically, the 'primal or originating layers' out of which present causes and effects arise. Ordeal, in this context, is literally what is 'originally dealt out to us,' the ur-deal, like the hand each person is dealt in a card game, out of which each person tries to shape a winning form as the game proceeds.

But of course, 'ordeal' also has the connotation of a very difficult, even cruel, pattern of experience we are subjected to, or choose to subject ourselves to in order to prove something to others and / or ourselves. I also see the ordeal as a knot or nexus of threads that come together in the weaving of our wyrd; something that challenges us and sets a pattern for the following phase of our lives, based on how we face and overcome (or fail to overcome) the ordeal. Ordeals are also, sometimes, a part of initiation activities in various contexts, sometimes appropriately administered, sometimes not.

Anyway, the point of this post is to ask anyone who wishes to respond about their perspective on what an ordeal is: an ordeal in general, and especially as it relates to fate / wyrd / orlog, both previous to and subsequent to the ordeal. I have the impression that there are multiple perspectives on what an ordeal is, and would benefit from reading any thoughts people might share here.

Thank you!
Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: The meaning of Ordeal
replies.content:
I eagerly look forward to this book, as I feel like there's a lot of depth to the heathen concepts of wyrd and orlog, but modern writers have only scratched the surface there.

I don't know that I had explicitly linked orlog and ordeal before, but that's certainly very interesting. I've been giving a lot of thought lately to a concept JMG discusses called "karmic culmination," which is an idea in some esoteric traditions that as you seriously take up spiritual work, any un-dealt-with karma comes a-calling and you have to deal with it in a quicker/more concentrated form than you otherwise might. Given how much of heathen esoteric work involves understanding and (perhaps) manipulating wyrd, it wouldn't surprise me if orlog would factor in in a similar way, and ordeal might be a part of that.

I also recently wrote up my thoughts on Galina Krasskova's He Is Frenzy, a large chunk of which describes nine ordeals (one for each of the worlds) that she underwent as part of her spiritual training. Some of these veered into territory I consider distinctly uncomfortable (self-flagellation, scarification, and such), but then, maybe the discomfort is the point, and of course, not everyone needs to undergo the same ordeals. In case it's interesting or useful: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_he_is_frenzy

I look forward to seeing what others have to say!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: The meaning of Ordeal
replies.content: From the perspective of the kind of occultism I study and practice -- which is of course a long ways away from Heathenry! -- the concept of the ordeal, especially in an initiatory context, is tangled up closely with the concept of karma. To begin a spiritual path, or to start a new stage in that path, requires facing your fears and your unfinished business; a formal ordeal, symbolic or otherwise, can kickstart that process into motion. Whether or not there's some such thing in a ritual of initiation, it's quite common for starting a new path or a new stage in an existing path to bring on various ordeals in the form of problems in daily life; this is what Manly P. Hall called "karmic culmination," and is the same process as it works out in a less formal setting.

replies.title: Re: The meaning of Ordeal
replies.content:
The idea of karmic culmination seems especially apropos to the ordeal...I mentioned the image of a nexus of orlog strands or patterns as one definition of ordeal: multiple strands coming together into a knot which must be addressed before proceeding further with one's wyrd. Sounds quite similar to the karmic culmination idea--thank you, Jeff and JMG!

Winifred

replies.title: Re: The meaning of Ordeal
replies.content:
This article may be of interest:
https://theomagica.com/blog/how-to-speed-up-your-magical-harvest

In it Frater Acher quotes Gustav Meyrink from "The Green Face":

"‘But then which purpose do all the punches of the outer life hold of which you are talking? Are they a test or punishment?’

‘There are neither tests nor punishments. The outer life with all its fates is nothing but a healing process, more or less painful for one or the other, depending on the way in which each person is sick in their reasoning.’

‘And you believe if I called God, as you say, my destiny will change ?’

‘Immediately! Only it will not change, it will be like a galloping horse that had been used to trotting so far.’"

replies.title: Re: The meaning of Ordeal
replies.content:
This is an interesting quotation, with the idea of fate being a healing process, which makes a lot of sense. And in one sense of the ordeal, it could play a role in that. The judicial ordeal, as practiced in medieval law, was often impossible to 'win' at, and cruelly implemented. But sometimes people chose to undergo an ordeal in order to prove their innocence, and when that proved successful it can be seen as a healing experience: healing the breach of law and community, reinstating the accused to a position of respect and acceptance.

Winifred

replies.title: Re: The meaning of Ordeal
replies.content:
A couple thoughts come to mind with this. One of the definitions of ordeal stems from a practice in which suffering was used to test innocence. I think it's interesting to view an ordeal as something that reveals one's character rather than makes it.

I'm struck by the word deal in ordeal, whether or not its etymologically important. I think it's interesting to think of an ordeal as something entered into, an experience that is agreed to rather than unwillingly endured. A classic horror trope: there is always someone at the outset who gives a clear warning and offers a way out, the protagonists are either dismissive, challenging, or naively oblivious. The ordeal starts when the path is chosen.

Wishing you well in your pondering!

replies.title: Re: The meaning of Ordeal
replies.content:
Thank you to all commenters for your thoughtful comments!

Winifred

replies.title: Re: The meaning of Ordeal
replies.content:
The ordeal as revealing one's character rather than making it...yes, though really it can do both. It's interesting you say that, because one of the important aspects of the ordeal in Heathen thought is that it reveals one's wyrd, when it comes as a test or in the form of combat. An event that is not a formal ordeal may be influenced by luck (good or bad), by chance, by other factors not immediately relevant to oneself. But a true ordeal, the nexus of orlog / wyrd, has nothing to do with luck or chance: the challenge and its outcome are revelations of one's wyrd, and one's character as you so rightly say! Your second paragraph is also very insightful--thank you indeed for these comments!

Winifred

comments.title: MOE Blessing Walk
comments.content:
I don't know if this will be of interest to anyone, but I thought I would post it anyway.
I've been involved with esoteric practices for over 40 years now, so I have a practice and lines of research I follow out and to which I stick fairly close. Recently this has led me to stuff JMG has written about, and very recently it led me to the MOE course. So far the only practice from this I have adapted is the Blessing walk, and, as I have said in other posts here, that practice alone has been worth the price of admission (which is nothing, actually. It's all posted here gratis, which is enormously and typically generous).
My job is a work from home deal, and I live in a rural subdivision in Illinois, so my Blessing walk started immediately with aiming blessings at trees and the local fauna (which is abundant), as opposed to people, since their aren't so many around during the day.
Please allow me to share a couple of things that have followed on from adapting this practice:
First of all, I feel like I know every tree and shrub in my neighborhood. "Being with" them is like stroking the flank of a horse: I don't know what their internal world is like, but I know they have an agenda, a raison d'etre, as important as my own, and I love them.
Secondly, animals I see, birds, chipmunks, snakes, deer, don't seem as inclined to flee from me as i walk by.
Finally, I have noticed that there is a different feeling when I just look at someone or something and and mentally say "May God Bless you" and when I *intend* it. It is a sense of something flowing out of the middle of my chest to embrace the person, animal or thing and it makes the blessing seem reciprocal.
Anyway, thank you for reading, and thanks for making this practice available.

comments.title: Magical Dating Advice

Hi JMG,

From a magical point of view, what would you advise to a shy and introvert man to find a good girlfriend for a long-term relationship?

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content:
Start by clearing away any emotional burdens that are blocking you. I recommend the Order of Spiritual Alchemy lessons, which you can begin downloading free of charge here:

https://octagonsociety.org/preliminary-lessons/

That will help get you clear of blame, shame, and guilt that may be feeding your shyness. Next you need to learn some kind of magic -- it really doesn't matter which kind. Magic is like music, it's not something you can do out of a book, and if you want to use magic to transform your life in this or any other way, you have to learn how to make it work. The mastery of will and imagination you get from the training will benefit you immensely in all aspects of life. Once you've got a solid grasp of your chosen system of magic, if you haven't already met somebody -- and it's quite possible that you already will have done so -- you can design and carry out an appropriate working to make it happen.

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content: [not op] Can you explain the difference between an ethical statement of intent for a love working versus an unethical one?  Would it be ethical to manifest meeting a love interest with specified qualifications?

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content: The thing to avoid above all else is focusing on any specific person. Be aware that the more tightly you specify things, the less likely the spell is to succeed -- on the one hand, there may be nobody in range who has exactly those characteristics; on the other, you may or may not be right when it comes to what you think you want...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Many of the Greek myths were reworked, sometimes many times over, to suit changes in religious tradition in Greece. Apollo was a latecomer to Delphi, and also a latecomer as an oracular god.  The oracle kept her old title, Pythia, "she of the python," after his priesthood and cult took over from the older serpent-worship there, which had once inspired the oracle.

comments.title: A question of Will
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I am currently reading Dorothea Brande's "Wake Up and Live!" I am certainly finding ways to root out failure in my own life and am reminded of the series of Will Exercises you have devised that can be practiced over the course of a few weeks, found under the Occult Training tag here on Ecosophia. I have found them helpful in the past but anticipate them being even more useful in combination with the information in Brande's book.

My first question is can I practice these will exercises alongside the LRM course? I am currently in the ninth lesson "Dimensions of Will" and want to make sure there is no conflict with this lesson or throughout the course.

My second question is whether exercises in previous lessons, specifically the memory palace exercise in the lesson on Chesed, can be practiced at anytime throughout the remaining lessons of the course?


Lastly I wanted to share an observation with you and the commentariat that occurred to me while practicing the self-perception exercise earlier in the course. I noticed that my proven weaknesses seem to in some ways correlate with proven strengths, as if they are really two facets of one underlying trait, or stated differently, are the same trait manifesting either as a strength or a weakness depending on the specific domain of life. I was curious whether you or others had observed this when performing the exercise.

All the best and thank you for this forum.

replies.title: Re: A question of Will
replies.content:
1) Yes, you certainly can.

2) Again, yes.

3) Three for three! Yes, we all have the vices of our virtues...
